question: Explain the fundamental architectural difference between a traditional RDBMS and Hadoop.
expectedPoints: RDBMS follows a centralized, shared-everything architecture with a fixed schema.
Hadoop follows a distributed, shared-nothing architecture designed for scale-out commodity hardware.
Hadoop uses a schema-on-read approach, allowing for flexible data storage and processing.

question: Identify one key scenario where an RDBMS would be a more suitable choice than Hadoop for data processing.
expectedPoints: When the application requires ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) transactions for data integrity.
When the workload involves frequent, low-latency updates to existing records (OLTP).
When the data is structured and fits well into a predefined, rigid schema.

question: Compare and contrast RDBMS and Hadoop across five key dimensions, such as data structure, scalability, cost, best-use cases, and data processing model. Provide a detailed analysis for each.
expectedPoints: Data Structure: RDBMS is structured/schema-on-write vs. Hadoop's any structure/schema-on-read.
Scalability: RDBMS scales vertically (scale-up) vs. Hadoop's horizontal scalability (scale-out).
Cost: RDBMS requires expensive proprietary hardware vs. Hadoop's use of low-cost commodity hardware.
Use Cases: RDBMS for OLTP and ACID compliance vs. Hadoop for batch processing and large-scale analytics.
Processing Model: RDBMS uses declarative SQL vs. Hadoop's procedural programming model (MapReduce/Spark).
